segments.s362-g41: This is a nominal predicate: 'جهان‌پهلوان' (world-champion) is the subject/predicative noun followed by the past copula 'بود' (was). 'آن روزگار' is a temporal adverbial ('in those days') with the demonstrative 'آن' preceding the time noun; word order (Noun + بود + temporal phrase) is standard in older Persian poetry.
segments.s362-g42: The clause begins with the subordinating/relative particle 'که' (that/when). 'کودک بد اسفندیار سوار' is a compact noun phrase: 'کودک' (child) modified by 'بد' (here pejorative/intensifying 'bad/fierce') and the proper name 'اسفندیار', with 'سوار' functioning as a noun 'horseman/rider' describing his state. The phrase is elliptical (no finite verb inside) and links back to the time expressed in the previous segment.
segments.s362-g43: Parallel nominal phrases linked by the copula: 'پناه سپه بود' = 'he was the refuge/protection of the army' ('پناه' + genitive 'سپه' + past verb 'بود'). The conjunction 'و' links this to the next nominal phrase 'پشت سپاه' ('the rear/support of the host'); word order places the possessed noun after the possessor (construct similar to English 'refuge of the army').
segments.s362-g44: A string of titles/nouns in apposition: 'سپهدار لشکر' = 'commander of the army' (title + host), followed by 'نگهدار گاه' where 'نگهدار' is 'guardian/keeper' and 'گاه' (poetically 'place/camp') completes the compound. In classical usage such nominal compounds are common and the verb copula is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s362-g45: 'جهان' is the grammatical subject. The prepositional phrase 'از بدی ویژه او' means 'from his special badness/harm' ('از' = from/because of, 'بدی' = harm/evil, 'ویژه' = special, 'او' = he/his). The verb 'داشتی' is an older poetic past form meaning 'had/possessed' (3rd singular here), so the clause says the world 'had' or 'experienced' this because of him.
segments.s362-g46: 'به رزم اندرون' is a locative/occasional phrase 'in battle/within combat' ('به' = in/towards, 'رزم' = battle, 'اندرون' = inside/within). 'نیژه او داشتی' continues with the same past verb 'داشتی' ('he had') and 'نیژه' (spear/javelin) as the object, so the whole means 'in battle he had his spear(s) at hand.' The verb is understood as continuing from the previous clause.
segments.s362-g47: 'جهانجوی' is the agent noun 'the ambitious/world-seeking one.' 'گفتا' is the past tense 'said' (classical form). 'به فرخ زریر' is the indirect object introduced by 'به' ('to/for') followed by an epithet 'فرخ' + name 'زریر' — a vocative/recipient of the speech.
segments.s362-g48: This continues the list of addressees: 'به فرخنده جاماسپ' = 'to Farrokhandeh Jamasp' (epithet + name) and 'و پور دلیر' = 'and the brave son' (conjunction + 'پور' = son, 'دلیر' = brave). The repeated 'به' at the start marks the indirect object the speaker addresses; the structure is a coordinated series of recipients.
segments.s362-g25: نیایش نمودند is a verbal phrase: نیایش (a noun 'prayer') + نمودند (literary past tense 3rd pl. of نمودن, 'to do/perform' here = 'they performed'). چون بندگان is a simile/adverbial phrase ('like servants') introduced by چون before the noun; the whole segment means 'they prayed as/like servants.'
segments.s362-g26: به پیش is a locative phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before.' گزین here functions as an epithet 'chosen' modifyingشاه ('king'); فرخندگان is an epithet 'fortunate/auspicious' agreeing as a descriptive element. Word order is demonstrative/epithet + noun (گزین شاه فرخندگان = 'the chosen, fortunate king').
segments.s362-g27: بدادندش is a single cliticized verb form: بدادند ('they gave') + ـش (to him). آن نامه خسروی is a noun phrase 'that royal/kingly letter' (آن = 'that/the', نامه = 'letter', خسروی = 'royal'). The verb precedes its object here in the narrative past construction.
segments.s362-g28: نوشته is used adjectivally/nominally as 'written'; درو is an archaic contraction (= در او) meaning 'in it' ('in that [letter]'). بر خط is the prepositional use 'in the hand/script of' (بر + خط = 'in the script'), and یبغوی is an attributive proper/ethnic adjective 'of Yabghu' (i.e. 'Yabghu-style script'). Together: 'written in it in the Yabghu script.'
segments.s362-g29: چو here = 'when' (a temporal conjunction). شاه جهان is a titled compound 'king of the world' (شاه + جهان). نامه را is the definite object ('the letter' + object marker را). باز کرد is a light-verb construction where باز ('open') + کرد ('did/made' = 'opened'). The sequence gives: 'When the king of the world opened the letter...'.
segments.s362-g30: برآشفت is a single past form meaning 'he was angered/grew agitated' (prefix بر- + آشفتن). و = 'and.' پیچیدن (infinitive/masdar here) + آغاز کرد is a compound meaning 'began to پیچیدن'; in context پیچیدن conveys restless turning/pacing about, so the clause reads 'he became angry and began to pace/turn about.'
segments.s362-g31: بخواند is a past/simple narrative form (here 'he summoned/called' — one of the dictionary senses). آن زمان sets the time 'at that time.' پیر جاماسپ را is the object phrase 'the elder (or venerable) Jamasp' with را marking him as the direct object of بخواند. Word order is verb + temporal phrase + object as common in epic narrative.
segments.s362-g32: کجا here functions as a relative/introducing word 'who/where' in older usage, introducing the subordinate clause describing Jamasp. راهبر بود گشتاسپ را is literally 'was (the) guide of Gushtasp' — راهبر = 'guide/leader,' بود = past 'was,' and گشتاسپ را marks Gushtasp as the one whom he guided. Together: 'who had been Gushtasp's guide.'
segments.s362-g33: A short nominal list in apposition: 'گزینان ایران و اسپهبدان' names two groups. 'گزینان' is a plural noun (the 'chosen' or 'select ones'), followed by the place/name 'ایران' and the coordinating conjunction 'و' linking to 'اسپهبدان' (spahbeds). The phrase has no finite verb here — a typical poetic nominal enumeration.
segments.s362-g34: 'گوان' here functions as a descriptive term attached to the following noun: 'گوان جهان‌دیده و موبدان' lists further groups/qualities. 'جهان‌دیده' is an adjective meaning 'worldly' or 'well-traveled' modifying an understood plural, and 'و موبدان' adds 'and the mobeds' (Zoroastrian priests). The whole line is a string of coordinated nouns/adjectives, typical of Ferdowsi's cataloguing.
segments.s362-g35: 'بخواند' is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb here with a classical/poetic form meaning 'he called' or 'he summoned.' It governs the following definite group 'آن همه آذران' ('all those Azar') and the prepositional phrase 'پیش خویش' ('before himself'); in Persian the verb often precedes or follows its object in poetry, and the enclitic 'ش' on 'خوی' (= خویش) gives the reflexive/possessive 'his own'.
segments.s362-g36: A continuation where 'بیاورد' (he brought) is another past verb applying to the scene; 'استا' is ambiguous here (possibly a name or an archaic term) and appears between verbs and objects in a poetic sequence. 'بنهاد' is another past/telic verb meaning 'he set/placed' or 'established.' 'پیش' repeats the sense 'before/in the presence of.' The two verbs form a sequence of actions performed by the same subject.
segments.s362-g37: 'پیمبرش را خواند' is a complete clause: the direct object 'پیمبرش' ('his prophet') is marked by the object marker 'را', and 'خواند' is the past tense verb 'he summoned' (here 'call/summon'). The conjunction 'و' links this clause to the next object phrase 'موبدش را', which is left without an overt verb — a common poetic ellipsis where the next verb will be supplied later or understood from context.
segments.s362-g38: This phrase contains the action of appointment: 'زریر گزیده سپهبدش را' uses poetic/inverted word order. 'گزیده' is a past participle/adjectival verb meaning 'chosen' (here functioning as the verbal act 'he chose/appointed'); 'زریر' is the person chosen, and 'سپهبدش را' (with 'را' marking the definite role/title) indicates the office 'his spahbed' as the role into which Zarir was placed. Poetic inversion places the name before the verbal adjective and the title after it.
segments.s362-g39: A simple copular clause: the name 'زریر' is followed by the title 'سپهبد' and the possessive 'برادرش' ('his brother') with enclitic 'ش', then the past copula 'بود' ('was'). The order places the person and title before the possessor phrase; 'بود' ties the predication together in past tense.
segments.s362-g40: 'که' introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding noun (explaining the brother/spahbed). 'سالار' ('commander') plus 'گردان' ('troop/unit') form the noun phrase 'commander of a troop', and 'لشکرش' ('his army') supplies the possessor with enclitic 'ش'. The clause closes with the past copula 'بود' ('was'); altogether: 'who was the commander of a troop of his army.'
segments.s362-g49: This is a relative/nominal clause headed by که (that/who). ارجاسپ is a proper name in apposition; سالار (title) follows the name in classical Persian order and qualifies ارجاسپ as a commander. ترکان چین is a genitive phrase where ترکان (the Turks) is specified by چین (= “of China”), so the whole string means “Arjasp, commander of the Chinese Turks.”
segments.s362-g50: یکی here means “a certain / one (a)”; نامه is the direct noun “letter.” کردست is an archaic perfect/past form (= کرده است → “has done/has made”), used here in the sense “has written/sent.” زی + من is the indirect-object relation “to me” in older Persian; زی is an older preposition not represented in the provided senses list (so marked -1). چنین is the adverb “thus/so,” completing what the letter said.
segments.s362-g51: بدیشان is composed of the prefix ب- (= to/for) + ایشان (them) and means “to them” or “before them.” نمود here is the past of نمودن and functions as a transitive past-tense verb “showed” (classical narrative usage). آن سخنهای زشت — ‘those ugly/vile words’ — is the object phrase; سخنهای is the plural/construct of سخن (“speech/words”).
segments.s362-g52: This is a relative clause introduced by که (“that/which”). نزدیک او here gives the idea of ‘to him / addressed to him’ (نزدیک often means ‘near’ but in this context has the idiomatic sense of being ‘at / before / to’ someone). شاه ترکان is ‘the king of the Turks’ (title + genitive), and نوشت is the past tense “wrote.” The clause therefore specifies who composed those words.
segments.s362-g53: The sequence is direct speech introduced into the narration. چه بینید is an interrogative “what do you see?” with بینید as the 2nd-person plural/present form of دیدن. گفتا is the past narrative verb “he said.” بدین is the prepositional phrase (ب- + دین) here functioning as “to these / to those” addressed, and اندرون means “inside / within,” so the whole unit reads “he said this to those within.” Note: دین as split here is an archaic form in the manuscript segmentation; its specific sense is not supplied in the senses list.
segments.s362-g54: This continues the quoted questions. چه گویید is another interrogative “what do you say / what is your counsel?” (گویید is the present/imperative form addressing the addressees). کاین را uses the archaic kاین (= “this”) plus the object marker را to mark the thing under discussion. سرانجام is a noun meaning “the outcome / end,” and چون is the question-word “how.” Together: “What do you say — how will this end?” Note: the provided senses for گویید in the prompt list do not include its verbal sense, so that entry is marked -1.
segments.s362-g55: This clause begins with the subordinating که (= “for/that”), giving an explanatory statement. ناخوش means “unpleasant / displeasing” here (poetic adjective). بود is the past-tense copula “was,” and دوستی is the noun “friendship.” با کسی means “with someone”; با is the ordinary preposition “with” and کسی = “someone / any person.” The clause states a general moral observation in past/gnomic narrative style.
segments.s362-g56: This relative clause describes that کسی: که introduces the relative ‘who/that.’ مایه here means “substance / means / resources” (i.e., intellectual substance or merit). ندارد is the present negative “does not have.” ز is the poetic preposition “from/of,” and دانش means “knowledge / learning.” بسی (classical adverb) means “much / abundant / sufficient.” The whole clause = “who does not have much learning.”
segments.s362-g57: This is an elliptical nominal clause with no overt verb: 'من' (I) + 'از تخمه' (from the stock/offspring of) + 'ایرج پاک‌زاد' (Iraj + the adjectival epithet 'پاک‌زاد' = 'noble-/pure-born'). The adjective 'پاک‌زاد' follows the proper name as a postpositive epithet; the whole phrase means 'I come from the noble-born stock of Iraj' with the copula ('am') implied.
segments.s362-g58: 'وی' (he/that one) begins a parallel nominal clause, again without an explicit verb: 'وی از تخمه تور' = 'he is from the seed/offspring of Tūr.' 'جادو نژاد' is a compound attributive: 'جادو' (magic) + 'نژاد' (lineage/born), meaning 'of magical birth/lineage.' The clause parallels the previous one (implied copula 'is').
segments.s362-g59: 'چگونه' (how) + 'بود' (was, past) introduces an interrogative/exclamatory clause; 'در میان آشتی' = 'in/among reconciliation' (i.e., 'between them, was there reconciliation'). Word order places the interrogative first; 'آشتی' is the noun 'reconciliation/peace'.
segments.s362-g60: 'ولیکن' (but/yet) contrasts the previous clause. The phrase 'مرا بود پنداشتی' is an older Persian construction: literally 'to me was a belief,' i.e. 'it seemed to me' or 'I believed it.' Here 'مرا' functions as the experiencer ('to me'), 'بود' is the past copula/existential 'was,' and 'پنداشتی' is used as 'belief/that which was thought,' yielding 'yet I supposed (so).' Note the clitic split in the tokenization ('م' + 'را').
segments.s362-g61: 'کسی کش' is a compound agentive noun ('کسی' = person/anyone + 'کش' = killer → 'one who kills people'). 'بود' is the past copula 'was'; 'نام' here means 'name' in the sense of 'reputation/fame'; 'و ماند بسی' ('and remained much') states that his name/fame endured to a large extent—'بسی' functions as an adverb 'much/greatly.'
segments.s362-g62: 'سخن گفت' is a nominal phrase meaning 'speech/conversation' or 'to speak'; 'بایدش' = 'it was necessary for him' where the enclitic '-ش' refers to 'him' (oblique pronoun). 'با هرکسی' = 'with everyone/anyone.' Taken together the clause means 'he had to speak with everyone' or 'it was necessary that he converse with anyone/everyone.'
segments.s362-g1: بپیچید is a past tense or narrative perfect form of پیچیدن used here to mean 'fold/roll (up)'; the initial ب- is the verbal prefix common in Classical Persian. The coordinating و links this action to the following clause; نامه is the direct object 'the letter', and بکردش (ب + کرد + ش) is a causative/past compound meaning 'he made/did to it' (here 'he made/put' a نشان). نشان is a noun 'mark/sign' describing what was put on the letter.
segments.s362-g2: This clause states the transfer of the item. بدادش as printed here does not match the supplied lemma senses (it functions as a past-tense verbal cluster 'دادش' = 'he gave it'); بدان is the prepositional/relational phrase 'to those' and هر دو گردنکشان specifies the recipients 'both those neck-throwers/rebels'. Grammatically, the phrase is 'he gave it to those two rebels' with هر دو modifying گردنکشان.
segments.s362-g3: بفرمودشان is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/told them' (ب + فرمود + شان). گفت here introduces reported speech ('he said'); بخرد is an attributive/adverbial form meaning '(the) wise (one)' or 'the wise', functioning as the object of the verbal instruction; بوید is the verb of the reported clause (here contextually 'seek/find' in speech) and appears in a present/subjunctive/narrative form used to report the command. Overall this segment reports: 'He commanded them, saying, "(go) to the wise (one) ...".'
segments.s362-g4: This is an imperative/subjunctive instruction of motion. به + ایوان او gives the goal 'to his iwan/portico' (به = 'towards/into'), با هم 'together' qualifies the participants, اندر + شوید is a classical compound meaning 'go/enter in' where شوید is a present-subjunctive/imperative form of شدن used here in an intransitive sense 'enter/become within'. The whole clause: 'Enter his portico together.'
segments.s362-g5: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; او را is the third-person object with the object marker را (here 'him'); ببینید is second-person plural present/subjunctive or historic present meaning 'you see' or 'when you see'. بر تخت و گاه places him spatially 'on the throne and the seat' (بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne', گاه here is a noun 'bench/seat' or 'place' in this poetic context).
segments.s362-g6: کنید is a second-person plural imperative/subjunctive of کردن meaning 'do/make' (here 'make/assume'); آن زمان 'at that time' (آن = 'then/that', زمان = 'time'); خویشتن را 'yourselves' with the emphatic/reflexive خویشتن plus object marker را; دو تاه is an archaic/formulaic phrase meaning 'twofold' or 'in two ways' (تاه here is the classical/formal element). The clause tells the addressees what to do at that moment: 'then make yourselves twofold/steady yourselves.'
segments.s362-g7: بر آیین شاهان is a prepositional phrase 'according to the custom/rite of kings' (بر = 'in/according to', آیین = 'custom/rite', شاهان = 'kings'). نمازش is 'his prayer' (نماز + possessive -ش), and برید is an imperative/past-inflected form of بریدن used here idiomatically to mean 'interrupt' or 'cut off' (i.e., 'break his prayer'). Grammatically this issues an order modeled on royal practice: 'According to kings' custom, interrupt his prayer.'
segments.s362-g8: This is a prohibitive clause. بر تاج و بر تخت او lists the objects 'his crown and his throne' with repeated بر ('on/at') for emphasis and parallelism; مگذرید is a negative imperative 'do not pass by/let (it) pass' (classical form used to command refusal to ignore). Together: 'Do not pass by his crown and throne' (i.e., do not neglect or fail to honor them).
segments.s362-g9: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. هر دو is a fixed phrase 'the two / both' (هر = each), and نشینید here is an archaic/formal past (or perfective) 3rd‑plural form meaning 'sat (down)'. در پیش اوی literally 'in his presence' (در + پیش = before/in the presence of; اوی is the pronoun 'he' with an enclitic vowel).
segments.s362-g10: سوی ... indicates direction 'toward'. تاج تابنده‌ش = 'the crown of his shining' where تابنده‌ش contains تابنده 'shining' plus the clitic -ش (= his/its). آرید is an imperative/jussive instruction 'bring/turn' (2nd plural hortative) derived from آوردن; روی is 'face'. Word order is somewhat inverted: 'bring/turn (آرید) [your] face (روی) toward the shining crown.'
segments.s362-g11: گزارید is a verbal imperative/hortative 'convey / report / deliver' (2nd‑pl form) from گزارْدن; پیغام فرخش را is the definite object 'the message of Farxš' marked by را. The verb precedes its object here as an instructive command.
segments.s362-g12: ازو 'from him / to him' marks the person to whom attention is given; گوش دارید literally 'you have ear' = 'listen / lend an ear' (گوش used as 'attention'); دارد/دارید is present 2nd‑person plural 'you have' used idiomatically with گوش. پاسخش را 'his answer' (پاسخ + possessive -ش) with را is the definite object being attended to.
segments.s362-g13: چو introduces 'when'. پاسخ ازو 'the answer from him'. سر بسر is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'through and through / in full / word for word' (frozen collocation); بشنوید is the 2nd‑person plural perfective/subjunctive form meaning 'you hear' or 'you have heard' in this temporal clause. Together: 'when you have heard his answer in full...'.
segments.s362-g14: زمین را 'the ground/earth' with را marks it as the direct object. ببوسید is an imperative 'kiss' (2nd‑pl). و links actions 'and', and بیرون شوید is a conventional imperative phrase 'go out / depart' where شوید is the 2nd‑person plural imperative of شدن used idiomatically with بیرون to mean 'leave'.
segments.s362-g15: چو 'when'. از پیش او 'from before him / from his presence' (از + پیش + pronoun او). کینه‌ور is a descriptive adjective 'resentful / spiteful' modifying the subject. بیدرفش (بی‌درفش) is a compound adjective 'without banner/standard' (a descriptive postmodifier). The three elements form a temporal clause describing his departure.
segments.s362-g16: سوی بلخ 'toward Balkh' gives direction. بامی here functions adverbially 'aloud / on high / on the roof' (i.e. raised up), and کشیدش is a past tense verb 'he drew / pulled it' with the enclitic -ش referring back to درفش (the banner). درفش 'banner / standard' is the object whose referent is indicated by the enclitic; overall: 'he drew his banner up toward Balkh.'
segments.s362-g17: This clause begins with the verb-form 'ابا' (here functioning as a past/preterite verbal idea 'he refused'), followed by the noun phrase 'یار خود خیره‌سر' (literally 'his own headstrong companion') and the compound predicate 'نام خواست' ('asked/wanted the name'). Word order is Verb - NounPhrase - VerbPhrase because classical Persian often permits the verb to appear earlier; 'خود' is reflexive/possessive 'his own' modifying 'یار', and 'نام خواست' is a light past-tense verb phrase meaning 'asked for the name'.
segments.s362-g18: The conjunction 'که' introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that / in order that'). 'او' is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he', and 'بفگند' is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'to cast/throw' (here subjunctive/optative nuance: 'that he might cast'). The noun phrase 'آن نکو راه راست' follows normal modifier order: demonstrative 'آن' + adjective 'نکو' + head noun 'راه' + adjective 'راست' — 'that noble/beautiful straight way'.
segments.s362-g19: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The preposition 'از' marks origin 'from', then 'شهر توران' is the place-name 'the city of Turan', 'به بلخ' gives the destination 'to Balkh', and 'آمدند' is the past plural verb 'they came'. Word order is typical: temporal word + origin + destination + verb.
segments.s362-g20: This short clause uses the preposition 'به' + place 'درگاه او' ('to his court/threshold') followed by 'بر پیاده شدند'. Here 'بر' + 'پیاده شدند' functions idiomatically: 'بر پیاده شدن' = 'to become on-foot' i.e. to dismount and go on foot; 'شدند' is the past plural auxiliary/verb 'they became'/'they did so'. The order is prepositional phrase then verb phrase.
segments.s362-g21: The clause starts with the adverb/adjective 'پیاده' ('on foot'), then the past plural verb 'برفتند' ('they went/proceeded'). 'تا' here marks extent/limit 'until/to' and is followed by 'پیش اوی' ('before him/in his presence'), where 'پیش' is a locative 'in front of / before' and 'اوی' is the enclitic pronoun 'him'. The structure shows motion culminating at the addressee's presence.
segments.s362-g22: 'براین آستانه' is a prepositional phrase 'upon this threshold' (demonstrative 'براین' + noun 'آستانه'), followed by the past plural transitive verb 'نهادند' (classical 'they placed/put') and the noun 'روی' meaning 'face' — idiomatically 'نهادند روی' = 'they laid down their faces' (i.e. they prostrated). The verb is classical past and the object 'روی' is literal 'face' used in a conventional gesture phrase.
segments.s362-g23: چو begins a temporal/simile clause 'when'. 'رویش' is the compound of 'روی' ('face') + the 3rd-person enclitic 'ـش' ('his'), so 'their seeing of his face'; 'بدیدند' is the past plural 'they saw'. The following short phrase 'بر گاه بر' repeats the preposition 'بر' with 'گاه' (occasion/time) for poetic emphasis — literally 'on the occasion/instant', an elevated word order common in epic verse.
segments.s362-g24: This is a simile introduced again by چو: two nouns 'خورشید و تیر' ('the sun and an arrow') are placed in comparison 'از بر ماه بر' — 'from/in front of the moon' with the preposition 'از' + 'بر'. The poetic ordering (repetition of prepositions and parallel nouns) is characteristic of Ferdowsi; interpretively it means the sight of his face was as striking as sun and arrow before the moon.

sentences.s362-s21: He was the world's champion in those days, when Esfandiyar, a fierce child, was a horseman.
sentences.s362-s22: He was the army's refuge and the army's rear (its support), the commander and guardian of the host.
sentences.s362-s23: The world suffered special harm from him; in battle he had javelins at hand.
sentences.s362-s24: The world-seeker said to Farrokh Zarihr, to Farrokhandeh Jamasp and to the brave son.
sentences.s362-s13: They offered prayers like servants before the chosen, fortunate king.
sentences.s362-s14: They gave him that royal letter, written in it in the Yabghu hand/script.
sentences.s362-s15: When the king of the world opened the letter, he became angry and began to pace (turn about).
sentences.s362-s16: At that time he summoned the elder Jamasp, who had been the guide of Gushtasp.
sentences.s362-s17: The select of Iran and the spahbeds, the gavan — worldly ones — and the mobeds.
sentences.s362-s18: He summoned all the Azar before him; he brought (it/one called) Asta and placed (it) before (them).
sentences.s362-s19: He called his prophet and his mobed, and he chose Zarir as his spahbed.
sentences.s362-s20: Zarir was his brother's spahbed — he was the commander of a troop of his army.
sentences.s362-s25: that Arjasp, commander of the Turks of China, has written (sent) to me a letter saying this.
sentences.s362-s26: He showed them those foul words that the king of the Turks had written to him.
sentences.s362-s27: “What do you see?” he said to those inside; “what do you say — how will this end?”
sentences.s362-s28: For friendship with someone who lacks much learning is unpleasant.
sentences.s362-s29: I am of the noble line of Irāj; he is of Tūr's line, born of magic.
sentences.s362-s30: How could there be reconciliation between them? Yet to me it was a belief (I had supposed so).
sentences.s362-s31: He bore the name 'killer of men' and his renown remained greatly; he was obliged to speak with everyone.
sentences.s362-s1: He folded/rolled it up and marked the letter, and he gave it to those two rebels.
sentences.s362-s2: He ordered them, saying, 'Seek the wise man'; enter his portico together.
sentences.s362-s3: When you see him on the throne and seat, then at that time compose/steady yourselves twofold.
sentences.s362-s4: In the manner/custom of kings, interrupt his prayer; do not pass by his crown and throne.
sentences.s362-s5: When the two had sat before him, turn your faces toward his shining crown.
sentences.s362-s6: Convey Farxš's message; lend him your ear and attend to his reply.
sentences.s362-s7: When you have heard his answer in full, kiss the ground and retire.
sentences.s362-s8: When, resentful, he left his presence without a standard, he drew his banner aloft toward Balkh.
sentences.s362-s9: He refused; his headstrong companion demanded a name, so that he would cast that noble, straight way.
sentences.s362-s10: When they came from the city of Turan to Balkh, they dismounted at his court.
sentences.s362-s11: They went on foot until they were before him; there upon that threshold they laid down their faces (i.e. prostrated).
sentences.s362-s12: When they saw his face, (it was) as when the sun and an arrow stand before the moon.
